request.question: Baby stars which American actor born on October 20, 1971?

title: Robert Lesser
Baby (2007 film)
Kenneth Choi
Henry James Montague
Robert Peters
Ethlyne Clair
Eli Nadel
Michael Romanoff
Mary Carlisle
Sasha Neulinger
sentences: Robert Lesser or Bobby Lesser (born October 22, 1942) is an American actor born in New York City and now living in Santa Barbara, California.
His earliest work dates back to 1967 with "David Holzman's Diary", directed by Jim McBride.
His latest movies include the Japanese film "Best Wishes for Tomorrow" and post-production "Painting in the Rain".
Lesser has worked with Bruce Willis, Ed Harris, Alan Arkin, Arnold Schwarzenegger, Matthew Broderick, Dennis Quaid, Penelope Ann Miller, and Richard Mulligan.
He is well known in the Santa Barbara theater community.
Baby is a 2007 independent film, considered part of the hood film genre.
The film tells the story of an Asian-American youth's gang life in East Los Angeles, set during the mid '80s to the early '90s.
Directed by Juwan Chung and starring David Huynh, Tzi Ma, Feodor Chin, Ron Yuan and Kenneth Choi.
It has been called "the Asian American Boyz n the Hood" by the San Francisco Chronicle.

The film won Best Narrative Feature at the 2007 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival and a Special Jury Award for "Outstanding Newcomer" for star David Huynh at the same film festival that year, and a Best Director award for Director Juwan Chung at the 2008 DisOrient Film Festival.
It was also distributed by Lionsgate.
Kenneth Choi (born October 20, 1971) is an American actor.
He is best known for playing the character Henry Lin on the television series "Sons of Anarchy" (2008–2014).
He is also known for his roles as Jim Morita in the film "" (2011), Chester Ming in Martin Scorsese's "The Wolf of Wall Street" (2013), Judge Lance Ito in "" (2016).
and Lewis on the FOX comedy series "The Last Man on Earth".
Henry James Montague was the stage name of Henry John Mann, (January 20, 1843 – August 13, 1878), an American actor born in England.
Robert Louis Peters (October 20, 1924 – June 13, 2014) was an American poet, critic, scholar, playwright, editor, and actor born in an impoverished rural area of northern Wisconsin in 1924.
He held a Ph.D in Victorian literature.
His poetry career began in 1967 when his young son Richard died unexpectedly of spinal meningitis.
The book commemorating this loss, "Songs for a Son", was selected by poet Denise Levertov to be published by W. W. Norton in 1967, and it still remains in print.
"Songs for a Son" began a flood of poetry.
Born Ethlyne Williamson, Ethlyne Clair (23 November 1904 – 27 February 1996) was an American actress.
She was a born in Talladega, Alabama.
A former New York art student, she appeared mostly in silent film, including several Westerns where she played the love interest to Hoot Gibson.
She was succeeded by Derelys Perdue in the "Newlyweds" film series, in which she appeared in 1927 and 1928.
She appeared in the serials "The Vanishing Rider" (1928) and "Queen of the Northwoods" (1929).
She was a WAMPAS Baby Stars alumna.
Eliah Maurice Nadel (October 9, 1918 – March 9, 1981) was an American actor born in the Bronx, New York.
Michael Romanoff, pseudonym for Harry F. Gerguson, born Hershel Geguzin, (February 20, 1890 – September 1, 1971) was a Hollywood restaurateur, conman, and actor born in Lithuania.
He is perhaps best known as the owner of the now-defunct Romanoff's, a Beverly Hills restaurant popular with Hollywood stars in the 1940s and 1950s.
Mary Carlisle (born February 3, 1912 or 1914; sources differ) is a retired American actress, singer, and dancer.
Raised in Boston, Massachusetts, she starred in several Hollywood films in the 1930s, having been one of 15 girls selected as WAMPAS Baby Stars in 1932.
Her first major role was in the 1933 film "College Humor" with Bing Crosby.
The two went on to perform together in two additional films, "Double or Nothing" (1937) and "Doctor Rhythm" (1938).
Carlisle retired from her acting career shortly after her marriage in 1942, with "Dead Men Walk" (1943) being her final film credit.
Sasha Joseph Neulinger is an American actor born on September 20, 1989, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Baby stars which American actor born on October 20, 1971?
hop1_docs: Eric Scott (actor) | Eric Scott (born Eric Scott Magat; October 20, 1958, in Los Angeles, California) is an American actor whose most noted role is as Ben Walton, which he first played in the movie "" in 1971, and subsequently in the series "The Waltons".

Chris Sligh | Charles Christopher Sligh (born July 20, 1978) is an American singer; songwriter and producer who was a finalist on the sixth season of "American Idol", finishing in tenth place.  He also starred in "October Baby".

Feodor Chin | Feodor Chin (born August 18, 1974) is an American actor, writer, voiceover artist, producer and director.  Chin starred as the antagonist "Benny" in Juwan Chung's "Baby" opposite David Huynh, the protagonist.  "Baby" won a Special Jury Award for Best Feature Length Film, Narrative at the 2007 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival, among other awards.  He also starred as pioneering actor and artist Keye Luke in Timothy Tau's short film bio-pic "Keye Luke", which premiered at the 2012 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival and which was also Closing Night Film of the inaugural 2013 Seattle Asian American Film Festival.  Chin also voices the character of Zenyatta from Blizzard Entertainment's game, "Overwatch".

Diana Serra Cary | Diana Serra Cary (born Peggy-Jean Montgomery, October 29, 1918), known as Baby Peggy, is an American former child actress, author and historian.  Although other child actors from the time are still living, she is the last living film star of the silent era.
Matthew Godfrey (actor) | Matthew Godfrey (born October 20, 1971 in Ottawa, Ontario) is a Canadian actor and voice actor.  He is a graduate of the National Theatre School of Canada.

Rajesh Kumar (actor) | Rajesh Kumar (born 20 January 1976) is an Indian actor, best known for his role as Subodh Thakkar in the Star Plus hit comedy-drama series "Baa Bahoo Aur Baby" and as Rosesh Sarabhai in Star One hit sitcom Sarabhai vs Sarabhai and the sequel . He is from Patna, Bihar and is an alumunus of Hindu College, University of Delhi.

Chad Willett | Chad Willett (born October 10, 1971, British Columbia) is a Canadian actor and producer who has worked for over 20 years as a professional in film, television and theatre.  His films include "Hector and the Search for Happiness", starring Simon Pegg, and "Monster Trucks" directed by Chris Wedge.
response.summary_1:
- Feodor Chin is described as "an American actor" who "starred as the antagonist 'Benny' in Juwan Chung's 'Baby'" and is given as born "August 18, 1974." 
- Matthew Godfrey is listed as "born October 20, 1971" (October 20, 1971) but is identified as a Canadian actor; he is not shown as part of Baby in these passages.
- Missing link: none of the passages identify an American actor born October 20, 1971 who starred in Baby. Next hop: retrieve the full cast/credits for Juwan Chung's film "Baby" (or search cast lists for any film titled "Baby") and check each cast member's birthdates to find any American actor born October 20, 1971.
hop2_docs: Juwan Chung | Juwan Chung (born 1983) is an American director and writer.  His gangland film "Baby" won a Special Jury Prize for Best Feature, Narrative at the 2007 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival and lead Actor David Huynh won a Special Jury Prize at the 2007 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival for Outstanding Newcomer and Best Emerging Actor for his performance in the film.  For "Baby", Chung also won Best Director at the 2008 DisOrient Film Festival.

Baby (2007 film) | Baby is a 2007 independent film, considered part of the hood film genre.  The film tells the story of an Asian-American youth's gang life in East Los Angeles, set during the mid '80s to the early '90s.  Directed by Juwan Chung and starring David Huynh, Tzi Ma, Feodor Chin, Ron Yuan and Kenneth Choi.  It has been called "the Asian American Boyz n the Hood" by the San Francisco Chronicle.  The film won Best Narrative Feature at the 2007 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival and a Special Jury Award for "Outstanding Newcomer" for star David Huynh at the same film festival that year, and a Best Director award for Director Juwan Chung at the 2008 DisOrient Film Festival.  It was also distributed by Lionsgate.

Feodor Chin | Feodor Chin (born August 18, 1974) is an American actor, writer, voiceover artist, producer and director.  Chin starred as the antagonist "Benny" in Juwan Chung's "Baby" opposite David Huynh, the protagonist.  "Baby" won a Special Jury Award for Best Feature Length Film, Narrative at the 2007 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival, among other awards.  He also starred as pioneering actor and artist Keye Luke in Timothy Tau's short film bio-pic "Keye Luke", which premiered at the 2012 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival and which was also Closing Night Film of the inaugural 2013 Seattle Asian American Film Festival.  Chin also voices the character of Zenyatta from Blizzard Entertainment's game, "Overwatch".

Puffkins | Puffkins is a series of spherical stuffed animals distributed by Lisle, Illinois-based Swibco.  It was introduced in 1997 as a competitor to Beanie Babies.  Puffkins are sold with a tag containing a birthdate and poem, and like Beanie Babies, the toys have new designs introduced and other designs retired.  Although Puffkins were retired in 2002, Swibco re-introduced the line as Puffkins 2 in 2007 with mostly the same design.
Juwan | Juwan is a given name.  It may refer to:

Juwan Simpson | Juwan Simpson (born Juwan Garth on July 8, 1984) is a Canadian football linebacker who is currently a free agent.  He recently played for the Calgary Stampeders of the Canadian Football League.  He was signed by the Green Bay Packers as an undrafted free agent in 2007.  He played college football at Alabama.

David Huynh | David Huynh (born February 5, 1983) is a Canadian actor.  Huynh won a Special Jury Prize Award at the 2007 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival for Outstanding Newcomer and Best Emerging Actor for his performance in Juwan Chung's "Baby", which also won a Special Jury Award for Best Feature Length Film at that year's Festival.  Huynh has also Starred in Byron Q's film, "Bang Bang" (2011), which won a Special Jury Award for Best First Feature, Narrative, at the 2011 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival.  He also played Jonathan Chen in Byron Q's film, "Las Vegas Story" and Ty Do in Mark Tran's film "All About Dad".  He recently stars in a Web Series entitled "Hollywood Aliens" from Beyond Cinema Productions, directed by "Bang Bang" Director Byron Q and Kevin Boston.  He also appears as the protagonist "Fong" in a pilot entitled "Chinatown Squad" about 1890s San Francisco Chinatown, directed and produced by Stephane Gauger and written by and starring "Baby" co-star Feodor Chin, who plays the antagonist, "Pistol Pete."  He has also starred as Benson Fong in Timothy Tau's short film bio-pic Keye Luke, which premiered at the 2012 Los Angeles Asian Pacific Film Festival and which was Closing Night Film of the inaugural 2013 Seattle Asian American Film Festival.
response.summary_2: Passages list Baby's principal cast as David Huynh, Tzi Ma, Feodor Chin, Ron Yuan and Kenneth Choi (passage 2), but none of the provided passages state any cast member's birthdate as October 20, 1971. The context's Matthew Godfrey (born October 20, 1971) is identified as Canadian and is not shown in the film's cast.
